data.title: Covista: A Mispriced Bet On The Healthcare Worker Shortage
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Covista Inc. (CVSA) (formerly Adtalem Global Education) has dropped over 30% since October due to short-term headwinds at Chamberlain University. While for-profit universities have been scrutinized and have failed previously, the shortage of healthcare workers and AI’s economic effects should allow Covista to see long-term growth. While the risks are persistent, I believe the current valuation gives investors a large enough margin of safety to justify a buy rating.

About Covista

Covista is a for-profit university company primarily focusing on healthcare training and professional development. It operates Chamberlain University (40% of revenue, 38% of operating income), which provides in-person nursing education programs. Walden University (39% of revenue, 45% of operating income) primarily operates online programs in nursing, education, psychology, and public health. Finally, their Medical and Veterinary schools (21% of revenue, 17% of operating income) include American University of the Caribbean School of Medicine, Ross University School of Medicine, and Ross University School of Veterinary Medicine. Combined, the company has over 90,000 students and graduates 10% of all nurses in the United States.

Recent Developments

Following the release of their Q1 2026 results in October, the stock dropped below $100 per share. While results did not disappoint and guidance was unchanged, a surprise Chamberlain execution error and Chamberlain management restructuring worried investors. As of their Q2 2026 report, all segments are seeing revenue growth, but Chamberlain reported a 1% YoY enrollment decrease, compared with the 1% YoY increase in Q1 and the 5.8% YoY increase in Q4 2025. Importantly, in the Q2 call, CEO Stephen Beard said,

Application volumes (at Chamberlain) for both pre-licensure and post-licensure nursing programs are up double digits during the second quarter, running ahead of where we were at this point last year.

This is a positive sign that Chamberlain has not lost its way, and management is backing it with aggressive campus growth planned. Revenue is still growing at each segment, and the Walden segment is seeing impressive results (27% revenue growth and 13% enrollment growth in Q2 2026), offsetting Chamberlain's near-term troubles.

While there may be short-term headwinds for Chamberlain, they are being more than offset by gains from the other segments. Additionally, several directors have purchased shares since the drop, and the company repurchased $165 million worth of shares in Q2. With a market cap of about $3.5 billion, this is impressive.

DCF Valuation For Covista

In their 2026 Investor Day, they reaffirmed their 2026 outlook and provided the following long-term guidance.

This business is not highly cyclical due to the federal funding it receives and the nature of the education market. While these targets are ambitious, there is a growing need for these services, and if government regulations stay relatively unchanged, Covista should continue to grow organically and through acquisitions to meet the projected healthcare worker shortage nationwide. For this reason, I will give Covista a 5% 10-year revenue CAGR, declining to 3% terminally. I project ROIC to increase about 100 bps, reflecting some operational improvements. I also project operating margins to increase to 20.5% due to the higher margin and Walden University’s rapid expansion. Finally, I am using a 7.61% cost of capital, with a 6.5% cost of debt, an 8.17% cost of equity, a 4.2% risk-free rate (10-year US Treasury), a 0.86 unlevered industry beta (0.99 levered), and a 4% equity risk premium. This will decrease to 7.5% terminally. My full model is below.

I got an intrinsic value of $136 for Covista, representing a 31% undervaluation at current prices. I believe I am being fair with most of my estimates. Assuming no dramatic regulatory changes, all of my terminal outcomes show a significant undervaluation. From this model, it is clear that the market is pricing in significant risk in these future cash flows.

Risks & Final Thoughts

While Covista is undoubtedly undervalued based on its cash-producing abilities, investing here has significant risks.

While AI is threatening many education companies, I believe Covista will thrive. Much of their business is in healthcare, a highly regulated industry where mistakes are costly. While the contents of the job may change, I do not believe the education, licensing, or regulation will. AI should level the playing field as it makes learning easier and thus opens the doors for more people to enter the industry. Additionally, as AI eliminates jobs, more people will need to switch careers, further increasing demand for Covista’s services. That being said, AI will likely increase competition and could threaten Covista’s competitive advantages, particularly the online programs of Walden, but it is difficult to replicate the brands, employer relationships, and scale of Covista's offerings. Overall, I believe that, with an aging population, a shortage of healthcare workers, and the effects of AI, the healthcare education industry will flourish.

During the 2010s, the for-profit university industry was struggling with several major universities losing their way, including Corinthian Colleges and ITT Technical Institute. These schools and Covista's Universities get much of their revenue from Title IV, so a termination of this accreditation would be detrimental. Below is the percentage of revenue each university gets from Title IV.

Anything above 90% is concerning and risks losing funding, and some of their schools are approaching this figure. With such a significant portion of revenue coming from federal funding and with regulations constantly being changed, this is the most significant risk for Covista. That being said, I do not see this happening in the current environment. As AI is threatening many jobs, I do not think the government will punish universities for helping people get great jobs. Especially when Covista graduates 10% of all nurses in the United States, limiting them would make the shortage worse. If anything, I believe that regulations could loosen as this issue worsens. If management continues to run their universities well, prioritizing student success, I believe Covista will thrive.

Covista is trading well below its intrinsic value, and while there are existential risks and there have been missteps in the past, I believe these risks are overblown. In an economy where people are struggling to find and retain employment and everyday AI is threatening more jobs, I do not believe the government will put more regulations on for-profit universities. Currently, I believe Covista will continue to expand profitably, so I am giving it a Buy rating.

data.title: IYM: A Solid ETF To Capitalize On Gases, Gold, Copper, Other Materials' Blistering Upside
data.description:
iShares US Basic Materials ETF (IYM) continues to make the most out of the basic materials uptrend while limiting a risk factor. In the last twelve months, IYM delivered a whopping total return of 35%, beating the basic materials indices and related ETFs by a significant percentage. The fund’s greater exposure to the metal and mining industry, along with its concentration in industrial gases and specialty chemicals, contributes to its upside. Its dividend factor is healthy, its expense ratio is low, and its liquidity is above the median of all ETFs. Therefore, I initiate coverage of IYM with a buy rating.

Basic Materials Earnings Growth Power Reflects Long-Lasting Uptrend

After years of underperformance due to the sluggish commodity prices, economic challenges, and higher rates, the US basic materials sector began to rebound over the past few quarters. The uptrend appears long-lasting amid a robust demand for metals, industrial gases, construction materials, and agricultural products.

According to the FactSet data, the basic materials sector is expected to generate 26% year-over-year earnings growth in 2026, the second largest growth in the S&P 500 sectors. The earnings growth momentum is expected to last for a longer time, given a mid-teen percentage growth forecast for the next year. The earnings growth has always been considered the biggest driver for investor sentiment, as it reflects the fundamental health of securities and sectors.

Year-to-date, the S&P 500 basic materials sector rallied 9% compared to the S&P 500 decline of 1%. The Morningstar US Basic Materials Index is also up 10%. Given strong earnings growth power, higher commodity prices, lower interest rates, and improving economic conditions, the sector is expected to generate solid returns for shareholders in 2026 and ahead.

How To Make The Most Out Of The Potential Uptrend?

There are various ways investors can access the basic materials sector. One of the most common ways to invest in the sector is to buy stock of companies. However, this strategy may not enable investors to fully capitalize on the sector’s broader growth potential. In addition, a single-stock investment carries a higher risk factor. I believe tracking the broader sector-focused indices may enable investors to fully capitalize on basic materials upside while limiting single-stock or industry-related risks.

Furthermore, iShares US Basic Materials ETF could be one of the best investment vehicles to make the most out of an uptrend while limiting a risk factor. In the trailing twelve months, IYM’s share price appreciated by 33%. Year to date, the fund’s shares are up 15%. It also offers a healthy yield. In the last twelve months, dividends contributed 2.5% to its total return of 35.5%.

Other popular ETFs focused on the basic materials sector are Vanguard Materials Index Fund ETF (VAW), State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB), and Fidelity MSCI Materials Index ETF (FMAT). All these ETFs have delivered solid returns in the last twelve months and year-to-date, reflecting a solid momentum across the industry due to the rising demand and higher prices. However, IYM stands out among peers due to its robust price and total returns. IYM earned a buy rating with a quant score of 4.18 based on the Seeking Alpha quant analysis. Whereas, other mentioned ETFs received hold ratings.

How Does IYM Offers Market-Beating Returns?

iShares US Basic Materials ETF’s performance is attributed to its targeted exposure to quality stocks in the fast-growing industries within the basic materials sectors. IYM seeks to track the performance of the Russell 1000 Basic Materials RIC 22.5/45 Capped Index by using a representative sampling technique. IYM’s portfolio comprises 42 stocks, with the top 10 holdings making up 67% of its entire weight.

According to the IYM’s factsheet, stocks from the industrial gases industry account for almost a quarter of its portfolio. In particular, Linde plc (LIN), IYM’s largest stock holding, represents 20% of its entire portfolio weight. Year-to-date, Linde generated 11% in price return as the largest industrial gas provider reported a record backlog of $10 billion as of the end of 2025, with a 6% to 9% earnings growth forecast for 2026. In 2025, the company’s earnings per share grew 6% year over year.

Air Products and Chemicals, Inc. (APD) is IYM’s second-largest holding from the industrial gases market. Like Linde, APD’s share price momentum accelerated amid better-than-expected financial performance. In the first quarter of 2026, its adjusted operating income improved 12%, while earnings per share increased 10% year-over-year to $3.16.

Besides industrial gases, IYM’s 18% portfolio tilt towards gold-focused companies continues to fuel its share price momentum. Year-to-date, the gold price surged 18%. In 2025, the yellow metal generated a historic annual price rally of 60%. Consequently, the gold-focused mining companies have also been delivering solid profits and returns for shareholders.

IYM’s top gold holdings are Newmont Corporation (NEM), AngloGold Ashanti plc (AU) and Royal Gold, Inc. (RGLD). In the last twelve months, Newmont’s share price surged 167%, while AngloGold’s price skyrocketed by 231%. Royal Gold’s share price increased nearly 90%.

In addition, IYM benefits from its healthy exposure to steel, copper, and other metals & mining companies, collectively making up more than 23% of its portfolio weight. The copper prices are up 26% in the last twelve months due to solid demand and tight supply. Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (FCX) is its largest holding from the copper market, accounting for 8.36% of its weight. Freeport shares soared 80% in the last twelve months. The company is also involved in gold and molybdenum production. Whereas, in the last twelve months, Steel Dynamics, Inc. (STLD) and Nucor Corporation (NUE), IYM’s steel-focused holdings, experienced a blistering share price spike of 50% and 34%, respectively.

Ecolab Inc. (ECL), Albemarle Corporation (ALB), and other specialty chemicals stocks represent 15% of its portfolio weight. The Dow Jones Specialty Chemicals index (DJUSCH) is up 11% in the last twelve months, with most of the acceleration coming in the past few months. IYM’s portfolio also offers a small exposure to distributors, paper and plastic packaging, and fertilizer and agricultural chemical industries.

Where Do Other Players Stand?

Vanguard Materials Index Fund ETF (VAW), State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB) and Fidelity MSCI Materials Index ETF (FMAT) delivered solid returns in the last twelve months. However, they have underperformed compared to IYM.

IYM’s price performance is attributed to its greater exposure to metals and mining stocks, including gold, copper, silver, and others. In addition, the industrial gases industry begins to rebound on improving earnings growth power, driven by the AI market and traditional industrial businesses.

Whereas, the Vanguard Materials Index Fund ETF tracks the performance of the MSCI US Investable Market Index (IMI)/Materials 25/50 by using a full replication technique. Its portfolio is diversified with exposure to around 117 stocks. VAW’s 10% of weight in gold, 6% in copper, and 7% in steel is less than IYM’s concentration in these metals. A low weight in hyper-growth gold, copper, and other metals is among the biggest reasons for its underperformance. The metals and mining industry is the fastest-growing industry in the basic materials sector. Whereas, the specialty chemicals stocks represent the largest weight in VAW’s portfolio.

The State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF’s shares soared 16% in the last twelve months, the lowest growth compared to other mentioned ETFs. The fund tracks the performance of 30 stocks, with 51% of the portfolio weight in specialty chemicals stocks. Whereas, the Fidelity MSCI Materials Index ETF is up 20% in the last twelve months. Its portfolio comprises 109 stocks, with the greatest concentration in specialty chemicals, industrial gases, and construction materials.

Risk Factors To Consider

The basic materials sector is a cyclical sector. Its performance is sensitive to commodity prices, economic trends, and geopolitical conditions. IYM’s greater concentration in the metals and mining industry increases its sensitivity to commodity prices. In addition, the fund’s exposure to industrial gases and specialty chemicals makes it vulnerable to economic headwinds. The geopolitical tensions in key markets can also negatively affect supply chain dynamics. The fund’s standard deviation of 17 is higher than the median of all ETFs of 10. Its price volatility of 21% indicates its potential to make the big price swings.

Final Thoughts

As the basic materials sector is expected to thrive due to the favorable market conditions, earnings growth power, and rising commodity prices, it is the time to make the most of the potential uptrend using investment vehicles, such as iShares US Basic Materials ETF. In addition to its exposure to industrial gases and specialty chemicals, IYM’s greater concentration in gold and other metals boosts its upside potential. The fund’s dividend factor is strong, with 5-year average dividend growth of 8.11%. Its expense ratio of 0.38% is lower than the median of all ETFs, while its average share trading volume of 141,000 is higher compared to the rest of the market.

data.title: Solid Power: Place Your Bets
data.description:
Back in December we first highlighted Solid Power, Inc. (SLDP) as a speculative buy. There was a really nice rally in shares following that buy call; nice trading gains were made in a short time. But all of those gains are now gone if profit was not taken.

In this column we will discuss some of the reasons for the reset, but we think at this $3 level, shares are once again at levels that we think investors should take a look at. Both long-term investors and those looking for a shorter-term trade. We still find the solid-state battery sector a fascinating sub-sector investment. It is still, however, a speculative corner of the market.

For Solid Power, we do reiterate that a lot has to go right. But the primary appeal of dabbling in the sector, in our opinion, lies in the massive leap in efficiency this technology offers over the status quo. As many of you reading this probably know, these units are designed to be safer, more durable, and capable of holding significantly more power over their lifespan than the lithium-ion cells that currently dominate the industry. While the applications are broad, the most obvious impact is in the electric vehicle space. As governments continue to push for an all-electric transition, the strain on the power grid remains a major hurdle. A long-term fix involves creating vehicles that simply require less frequent charging. For many consumers, the fear of being stranded without a nearby charging station keeps many from buying electric. While more and more stations are being built, they are exponentially less frequently found than traditional gas stations. Solid Power is one of several names looking to carve a niche in this space and scale up their offerings.

Look, there is no doubt that we are still very much in the early stages of this technological shift, but the long-term investment potential is hard to ignore. We are reiterating this high-risk, high-reward play in this sub-sector that fits the profile of the speculative ideas we occasionally feature in our specialized room at BAD BEAT Investing. Why has the stock been selling off so much? The sell-off began in January and is a result of a mix of technical dilution, rising bearish sentiment, and a broader market shift away from speculative tech. The biggest reason has got to be the $130 million registered direct offering in late January. While management spun this as a way to strengthen their balance sheet and extend their runway (which it certainly does help), the market viewed it as significant dilution. The offering included a massive amount of common warrants. By doing this, it kind of put a ceiling on the stock price in the short term, as investors reacted to the sudden increase in the potential share count. We also saw short interest creep up to the mid-teens as a percent of the float right around that same time, which suggests, of course, that a growing number of traders were actively betting against the company as it struggled to maintain its December and January momentum that we enjoyed following our buy call.

Frankly, we thought that the earnings report might help the stock stabilize, but the fundamental picture the report didn't do much. While the company technically beat the earnings per share figure, that does not really matter to investors at this point. While the company is managing its burn rate carefully while pushing hard on technical milestones, the report was met with mixed reactions. Revenue for the year was a paltry $21.7 million, which was a slight uptick from the previous year, mostly driven by their ongoing work with SK On. The net loss for the year was $93.4 million, but at this stage of the game, liquidity is everything. The company ended the year with $336 million in cash and securities, which was a reason the market questioned the timing of the $130 million offering. But it's in the past, and so the cash position has been boosted nicely. For the next few years, the company has a nice runway to fund operations without immediate panic, even as management projects spending between $85 and $100 million throughout 2026. Barring some sort of unexpected new deal, the next big catalyst to watch will likely be the Q1 2026 earnings call currently slated for early May, where we should get an update on how the company’s pilot line installation is progressing and if the partnership with SK On has reached its final acceptance milestones.

The technical milestones with BMW and SK On are moving forward, but some $93 million in annual losses and a projected cash burn of up to $100 million for 2026 are just weighing heavily on the narrative. In an environment where the market is hyper-focused on when these early-stage companies will actually become self-sustaining, a supplier-only model that is still years away from meaningful commercial revenue is keeping folks on the sidelines. But this is where we think buying the erosion in shares is a contrarian play. Catalysts are more deals, more milestones hit, and expanded partnerships. Good news on those fronts will likely result in buying pressure and short-covering. Some also generally question whether solid-state technology is truly a game-changer or just another high-risk venture gamble that might be overtaken by other battery innovations. So, it is speculative.

On top of the company-specific issues, the macro environment has been pretty hostile to growth names like this lately. With geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and fluctuating energy prices giving rise to market malaise, we are seeing investors pulling capital out of speculative industrials and parking it in more stable, value-oriented sectors. This broader market weakness has further pressured Solid Power to underperform even the general industrial ETFs, as any sign of execution risk becomes magnified when the overall market is nervous. So, the selloff reflects how the excitement over an early-stage company gets a partnership announcement, boosting the stock, but then it wears off and the long road to commercialization becomes the primary focus again. But at these levels, and with the bad news weeks and weeks in the past, shares have likely passed the worst.

We think the appeal of the company persists for investors. Instead of competing in the capital-intensive world of high-volume manufacturing, the company is positioning itself as a critical supplier of the underlying technology and materials. Yes, they are a ways away from getting to big commercialization, and there is no guarantee they get more deals or expand further. Remember the company's primary product is a sulfide-based powder designed to replace the liquid electrolytes found in traditional lithium-ion batteries. They sell this material to other battery manufacturers who then integrate it into their own solid-state builds. Beyond material sales, they generate revenue by licensing their specific designs and manufacturing processes while also providing engineering services to help automakers set up their own production lines. It is a niche approach that could allow them to build a significant moat, but keep in mind they are certainly not alone in the race. Given that we are still in the very first innings of their growth story, a lot still must go right. But if it does and the technology gains widespread traction, the long-term upside is significant.

On the operational side, there are a few specific things to watch for moving forward. The company is currently on track to commission its continuous electrolyte production pilot line by the end of this year. This is a massive step because it moves them from laboratory-scale proof to industrial-scale manufacturing capability, aiming for an annual capacity of up to 75 metric tons of that sulfide-based powder. The company is also deep into site acceptance testing at SK On’s facility and has been actively providing electrolyte samples to Samsung SDI and BMW. These partnerships are currently everything for the company right now. They have to go well. If so, we can see the company move from conceptual agreements to actual commercialization. This would be the setup for the moat the company is trying to build. At $3, it's speculative, but there are catalysts to move shares higher this year and longer-term.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Novo Nordisk - Enough Is Enough: 5 Reasons It's Time To Sell (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview: Understanding Rationale For A Sell Call On Novo Stock Part 1 - Semaglutide's Meteoric Rise Was Unsustainable

First, let me preface this analysis by stating that I hold some Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) stock, acquired at around this time last year - and in the 12 months since, my position has halved in value.

Second, a quick check on Seeking Alpha tells me that only six of the past one hundred notes published by contributors to this site on Novo Nordisk stock have been Sell or Strong Sell calls - kudos to this lone voice in the wilderness.

It is obvious why so many analysts believed - and may well still believe - that each drop in the value of Novo Nordisk represents a buying opportunity, although when we truly think about it, we should note that the bull thesis essentially comes down to a single drug molecule - semaglutide.

As we all know by now, semaglutide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist that secured its first approval in December 2017, brand name Ozempic, indication, Type 2 Diabetes. In a statement released at the time, Novo stated:

The approval of OZEMPIC® is based on the results from a Phase 3a clinical trial program. In people with type 2 diabetes, OZEMPIC® showed clinically meaningful and statistically significant reductions in A1c compared with placebo, sitagliptin and exenatide extended-release.

As a secondary endpoint in the trials, treatment with OZEMPIC resulted in reductions in body weight. The most common adverse reactions reported in ≥5% of patients treated with OZEMPIC are: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain and constipation.

Semaglutide was, in fact, able to trigger healthy weight loss that far exceeded what the scientific community had thought possible up until then, and in June 2021, it was approved to treat obesity under the brand name Wegovy, based on results from its pivotal STEP1 study, which showed that:

patients taking Wegovy achieved an average weight loss of 14.9% of body weight at 68-weeks vs. 2.4% for placebo. In addition, 83.5% of patients achieved 5% or more body weight reduction in the Wegovy arm vs. 31.1% for placebo.

Analysts originally heralded the arrival of an anti-obesity market that they believed could exceed $130bn per annum in peak revenues by 2030, and Novo's new products did their best to support such a thesis. In 2024, Ozempic earned ~$16.8bn of revenues, and Wegovy~$8.1bn, and last year, these figures rose to ~$20bn, and $12.5bn respectively.

Glucagon-like peptide 1 ("GLP-1") is a hormone associated with hunger, appetite and food cravings, and by mimicking its effects, Wegovy can reduce hunger cravings, stimulate insulin secretion, lower glucagon secretion, and delay gastric emptying.

Almost exclusively due to this "miracle" weight loss drug, Novo Nordisk's share price increased in value from $35 per share in March 2021, to >$140 per share in July 2024. Its market value rose >$570bn, which analysts were quick to point out, made the Danish Pharma more valuable than Denmark's entire economy.

Understanding Rationale For A Sell Call Part 2 -Semaglutide's Horror Year In 2025 Gets Worse In 2026

With Wegovy destined to become one of the pharmaceuticals industry's all-time best-selling drugs, most analysts believed Novo stock would simply keep rising - after all, the World Health Organisation (WHO) estimates there are 890m people worldwide living with obesity, and Wegovy cost ~$1,200 per month. The market opportunity could therefore be considered to be >$1 trillion dollars.

In that context it therefore seems astonishing that Novo stock today trades at a value of $38 - down >70% since its July 2024 high. What has happened?

As with most crises, it is rather an accumulation of unfortunate circumstances than one major issue. As I previously mentioned in an article published in September last year (advising investors to "Hold Firm"), in December 2025, Novo's Cagri-Sema drug, a combination of cagrilintide, a dual amylin and calcitonin receptor agonist, and semaglutide, failed to live up to management's promise it would achieve >25% weight loss in patients. I further stated:

In April, Novo stock lost value as President Trump announced "Liberation Day" tariffs on countries importing products into the U.S. - which would likely affect Novo given it is an overseas company (albeit one with plenty of manufacturing capacity in the U.S.).

In May 2025, Novo downgraded its full-year 2025 guidance, blaming companies selling "compounded" (non-FDA approved) versions of its drug by exploiting certain loopholes around supply scarcity and "personalised" doses.

This resulted in the departure of Novo's CEO, but in July, Novo downgraded guidance once again, with sales growth now expected to be 8–14% and operating profit growth expected to be 10–16%.

Next, Novo reported it would "streamline operations" and lose a chunk of its workforce, resulting in restructuring costs of around DKK 9 billion (~$1.4bn), downgrading operating profit growth still further, to 4-10%.

To update since that note. in September last year, stock traded ~$54, but it has lost another 30% since, and this despite the fact the company secured the approval of an oral version of Wegovy (a once weekly self-injectable) earlier this year.

The bad news has simply kept coming - in November, Novo agreed to slash the cost of Wegovy / Ozempic from >$1k, to ~$350 when purchased via the government's DTC platform TrumpRx, and to offer its new oral pill for just $150 per month.

Novo lost out to Pfizer (PFE) in a bidding war for promising GLP-1 drug developer Metsera, oral Ozempic failed to slow the progression of Alzheimer's in a clinical study, and the FDA issued a warning letter over conditions at a drug manufacturing facility in Bloomington, Indiana, acquired as part of Novo's ~$16bn buy-out of drug manufacturer Catalent.

Novo cut the price of Wegovy in China and India, Amazon (AMZN) began to offer the Wegovy pill for as little as $25 per month, studies showed that patients may regain weight rapidly after treatment with GLP-1 drugs, and generic drug maker Sun Pharma won the right to sell a generic version of semaglutide in India.

2025 results disappointed the market, with revenue growth of just 10% year-on-year, and operating profit growth of just 6%, and the 2026 outlook also shocked Wall Street - sales and operating profit growth of -5% - 13%. Novo threatened to sue Hims and Hers (HIMS), the telehealth platform offering compounded versions of the Wegovy pill (the two are not said to working on a new partnership, after a disatrous first attempt), and the FDA accused Novo of making "misleading claims" in its latest TV commercial.

Cagrisema's data disappointed again, and in February, Novo posted its worst ever monthly stock price performance, with stock falling 37%. Goldman Sachs (GS) and JP Morgan (JPM) analysts have both downgraded Novo stock this year, research has suggested Ozempic generics could cost as little as $3 per month, and finally, yesterday, Seeking Alpha reported:

Novo Holdings A/S, the investment firm that controls Novo Nordisk (NVO), said Thursday that its assets under management fell to about DKK 694B (€93B) last year from DKK 1,060B (€142B) in 2024, primarily reflecting the significant decline in the market value of Novo Nordisk (NVO).

The decline was compounded by the drugmaker’s decision to halt share buybacks and by the U.S. dollar weakening against the Danish krone, which further pressured returns.

Not just a perfect storm, but perfect storms, plural - and then we must consider arguably the biggest problem Novo has - its almighty competition.

Understanding Rationale For A Sell Call Part 3 - The Unstoppable Rise Of Eli Lilly - & Emergence Of New Competition

For some years now analysts have made the bull case for Novo by telling investors to just look at Eli Lilly (LLY) stock, that's the bad buy opportunity, the valuation is too high - look how low Novo's valuation is!

Lilly is the other major beneficiary of the rise and rise of GLP-1 drugs. Its tirzepatide molecule is in fact a glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) mimetic rolled into one, and, branded as Mounjaro in diabetes, and as Zepbound in obesity, it has begun to take over the marketplace.

As we can see in the slide above, taken from Novo's 2025 results presentation, its share of the GLP market is declining, and in 2025, tirzepatide outsold semaglutide. Mounjaro earned $20.8bn, while Ozempic earned ~$19.6bn, and Zepbound earned $12.5bn, while Wegovy earned ~$11.8bn. This despite Mounjaro / Zepbound being approved years later.

Confounding many people's expectations that Lilly stock would fall as Novo's rose, Lilly stock has simply kept growing (albeit, having achieved the $1 trillion dollar market cap valuation mark, it has now plateaued somewhat).

Why is this? The answers are not too hard to find. It's not enough for a pharmaceutical company to have a great drug. It has to manufacture the drug, distribute it, market it, protect its patents, and develop even better drugs.

Novo scores quite poorly on all these fronts. Its Catalent acquisition has proved troublesome, yet its biggest market by far - the U.S. - is demanding it manufacture its product in the U.S. or face tariffs on imports into the country.

Eli Lilly expects to deploy $27bn building new manufacturing plants in the US - a sum Novo simply can't compete with. In fact, it was reported yesterday that Lilly plans to invest $3bn in manufacturing in China also. Lilly's revenues were up 43% in 2025, and management expects them to grow by 25% in 2026.

It seems as though Lilly has its foot on Novo's throat and has no intention of removing it - ultimately, unless Novo is able to produce an even more miraculous drug than semaglutide, we may argue that whoever controls manufacturing controls the GLP-1 market, and at present its clear that company is Lilly, and that Novo is not in possession of a superior drug to semaglutide.

In fact, the Pharma companies Merck (MRK), Pfizer (PFE), Amgen (AMGN), Astrazeneca (AZN), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) and AbbVie (ABBV) are all developing GLP-1 drugs, and could even replace Novo as Lilly's biggest competitor by the end of this decade, I'd speculate.

Semaglutide and tirzepatide patents seemingly expire around the same time, early in the next decade, in key markets such as the US, which is an issue that could undermine both companies, in my view, or could it be that manufacturing cheap GLP-1 drugs proves economically unfeasible given GLP drugs are no longer very expensive? In that scenario, with its superior infrastructure, once again, Lilly likely wins that battle.

Understanding Rationale For A Sell Call Part 4 - It's Not The Case That Good News Always Follows Bad

I am as guilty as any analysts of preaching the "Buy the Dip" thesis in relation to Novo for the past 18 months, but mainly because of the incredible market opportunity I believed Novo would always be an active participant in, thanks to its long history of treating diabetes, and its leading role in the development of GLP-1 drugs.

But as mentioned, without manufacturing and infrastructure support it becomes harder and harder to stay competitive in a market that is no longer about drug development, but more about making and selling.

I had formerly compared the relationship between Novo and Lilly with Pepsi and Coke - the two competing companies are good for one another, stimulating interest and positive competition - but Novo is arguably at risk of losing its "Pepsi" status to another leading global Pharma.

Novo has now lost its first-mover advantage, and the management team that helped achieve it. I made the case in a recent note that new CEO Maziar Doustdar had impressed me with his pledges to take the fight to Lilly, and reposition Novo for success, but I am increasingly thinking that issues at Novo run so deep, no one individual can effect positive change.

My point, then, is that analysts have made a Buy thesis out of the idea that when something bad happens to a company, something good will shortly come along to revive the company and the share price. This is not necessarily the case, however, and looking ahead, I can't see, blind faith aside, how Novo arrests the slide in its business and wins back investors.

Understanding Rationale For A Sell Call Part 5 - Institutional Investors Are Likely To Exit Positions

My final point is arguably the most important point. As retail investors, we are in thrall to institutional investors. If a major institutional investor opts to start selling their stake in a Pharma company, no amount of retail investors "buying the dip" and accentuating the positives will reverse the decline in the share price.

Novo Holdings apparently owns ~28% of Novo Nordisk, and Novo has been losing the company money, as discussed above. Blackrock reportedly owns ~4% of stock, the Vanguard Group 3.25%, and many other investment houses - JP Morgan, Deutsche Asset Management, State Street Investors, own ~0.5%. Novo Nordisk also reportedly has a troubled boardroom, so even if there were ways to reverse Novo's decline, these opportunities may be missed amid the boardroom shenanigans.

In summary, as ever, if we simply take the GLP-1 molecules semaglutide and tirzepatide, and note how closely they match one another in both a clinical and a real-life setting, and in terms of sales, then consider Lilly's market cap - $933bn at the time of writing - and Novo Nordisk's market cap - $168bn - it is hugely tempting to conclude Novo is the screaming buy opportunity. After all, you could easily double or triple your money once Novo's valuation catches up with Lilly's.

Scratch under the surface, however, and it becomes more and clear that Lilly has the momentum, and that Novo's problems are mounting. That, in a nutshell, is why Novo stock is not actually cheap, and Lilly stock not actually expensive.

As such, in my view, and of course, my opinion may be misguided, there is a no logical reason to believe Novo's stock has any upside potential at the present time (forward price to sales trending close to 4x does not really suggest undervaluation), but several reasons - discussed above - to suggest at the end of 2026, Novo's share price will be lower than it is today.

(I have not exited my position in Novo yet, but I suspect that continuing to hold is the naive move, not the smart move to make at this time.)

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The New Value Stocks
data.description:
This is not the first time I've discussed shifts in market dynamics from favoring asset-light to asset-heavy businesses. Consider the discussion in this article to be a continuation of the ideas presented in this one, “The CapEx Increase Is Bullish,” where I argue that this shift will be structural and ultimately favor Big Tech, as they increasingly are becoming asset-heavy businesses.

This comes at a time when the market is rewarding asset-heavy businesses like software never ate the world.

I've been covering this trend and calling it a rotation, but I've come to a new conclusion about all the CapEx spending and this market regime: there will be new value stocks.

The market can buy capital intensity and hyperscaler tech firms.

Let me square that for you, but first, here's an excerpt from that past article to get you up to speed:

Consider the terror growth stocks have had over value stocks for the past two decades and how that's lined up with the changes made to the economy and monetary policy since post-2008.

This shift (to asset-light businesses came) at the same time as software began to eat the world with its ease of proliferation...

By incentivizing and moving to buybacks and accounting gains over the production of physical goods or the development of land, domestic investment fell off trend.

The post-2008 changes were just the last straw on the camel's back.

Domestic investment never came back to trend, and the outperformance of value by growth became a given to most investors as the economy became less about capital investment to spur growth and instead turned to low-marginal-cost software (the cost to sell an additional copy of Windows is far, far less than the cost of making Windows itself).

The rotation back into these companies will come after they can demonstrate their chops in this new environment, and so I start to understand why Big Tech is selling off on this news. The market is unsure if they can keep up their high margins and profitability in this new dynamic. I believe they can.

Well, a month later and it's still ongoing. First came the actual value stocks, and their rotation has been duly noted as well, then small caps, and soon, back to Big Tech as they finish their transitions to being far more asset-heavy than they are now. That will happen over the coming years, as trillions are borrowed and data centers are built out all over the planet. I expect the market to price that in far sooner than years, however, as it is a forward-looking beast.

It's already being priced in to some degree.

Consider that since the year began, we've been seeing a clear break between Big Tech (Nasdaq 100 as a proxy) and infrastructure and energy firms. This is even true of data center and AI infrastructure firms, which have been wildly outperforming the model providers (“GS AI”).

A Return To Value

When I say “value” here, I really do mean that I think traditional value firms have a better shot moving forward than they had in the past. The dynamics of markets are different, and what investors want today is far different from what they wanted in the last few years.

Consider that the dynamics of private and public debt completely flip post-GFC. It was the result not only of the massive spending that took place to build out the houses that ended up in a disastrous bubble, but also because after the pop, investors were facing a zero-interest-rate policy (“ZIRP”). This meant yields were at historic lows following the blowup of many capital-intensive businesses.

What do you do when bonds pay next to nothing? You buy risk assets.

The flip this time around was sudden, but asset-heavy firms are now trading at a premium to asset-light firms. We could be seeing the end of a 25-year trend. The only other times this has broken were during historic economic downturns like recessions, which we are not in right now.

CapEx Is The New Cool

If we're moving to a new era of capital intensity that could give more credence to value stocks, then we have to discuss the elephant in the room: some growth stocks are becoming value stocks.

One of the interesting shifts to watch as this takes place is that Big Tech/hyperscaler firms (so, exclude Apple et al. in this) are becoming closer to value stocks in their valuations. As they pile on more and more debt, the market is changing its opinion about these firms.

That doesn't make them less desirable for the long run, but consider that right now, Microsoft (MSFT) is being given the same forward PE ratio as Coke (KO). But one of these businesses is still largely deriving its growth from low-marginal-cost software.

It has to do with all the spending they intend to do, with Google (GOOGL) issuing 100-year bonds, Amazon (AMZN) ranking in the top 5 high-grade issuances ever, and the other hyperscalers following similar lines to fund their massive spending sprees.

But this is a transition period, and one that will bring with it opportunities for investors to own the capital-intensive workhorses of the next market regime.

If investors want hard assets, these Big Tech firms are certainly building them up. It will take time, but the transition is absolutely underway and now being financed in full force.

This is what I believe is really triggering the current rotation that will eventually broaden to include these hyperscalers: investors are watching the actual issuance of these bonds and want to make sure demand exists before they bid these back up to where they once were. These firms were talking big about spending, but now they are actually selling 50-year and 100-year tranches of bonds to fund their spending. Google will be paying for these data centers until 2126. That makes it far more real, and it's understandable that investors have been eager to rotate into energy and infrastructure.

Yields are hard to come by right now, and so naturally, these issuances went well and were met with healthy demand. That is the kind of confidence boost Big Tech needs to keep seeing. Consider that their competition is fairly slim from the fixed income world; nearly 90% of bonds currently outstanding yield less than 5%.

So I can absolutely see a world in which hyperscalers transition more of their valuation metrics to be in line with traditional value stocks due to this borrowing and spending but are still bought up at the end of the day.

This may make value stock indexes more attractive; many of them are market cap weighted, and so if hyperscalers start qualifying for their inclusion, they would immediately take up large amounts of the fund. On the other hand, this would likely cause them to be sold by growth funds, so I don't think the buying pressure is too much of a catalyst in net.

These firms still have fundamentals that keep them locked in growth indexes for now, but these figures are falling. We'll see how far they can sink; the bottom may not be until a few months from now.

Not All Hyperscalers Are Made Equally

It should be clear to readers at this point that I am bullish on these firms, and I, personally, have positions in MSFT, GOOGL, and AMZN that I am adding to on their recent weakness.

The keen-eyed among my readers will have noticed that I have not mentioned Meta (META) yet, although they are also a heavy issuer of debt for AI spending. This is because I am less bullish on their AI business; their model, Llama (the flagship; they run several), is effectively never brought up in conversations around top AI models. They just announced a delay, citing poor performance.

There is also a concern of firms like META employing creative financing to keep the debt off their books. This can hide some debt, which makes the business look different from a valuation perspective. Be wary of these kinds of deals; I expect them to become more and more popular with hyperscalers, especially those not as cash-rich as MSFT and GOOGL.

Conclusion

While I am bullish during this downturn and see this rotation into traditional value stocks as a prelude for a realignment of what a “value stock” is, we may soon include some names that would've had investors of a few years ago laughing. I am not sold that a return to capital intensity means that we will see the dominance of energy and industrials over tech.

Instead, I see tech—Big Tech hyperscalers in particular, as pure-play software is still having its own woes—as being now in the middle of a transformation into value stocks. We may soon see these firms I've mentioned, the ones with the largest borrowing and willingness to spend on AI, start to qualify for inclusion in value-oriented funds. I would not be surprised if they then start driving the returns for those funds.

That is to say, in the simplest way I can, value indexes outperforming and hyperscalers outperforming can be inclusive of each other. The hyperscalers seem to be moving themselves in that direction due to all their borrowing and spending.

It will take time, and there may be more downside ahead of us as the transition continues. But I don't believe that this shift will kill the long-term prospects of these hyperscaler firms.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Signet Jewelers: More Than Engagements Under The Surface
data.description:
Introduction

As recently as 2024, Signet Jewelers (SIG) was under pressure from a post-pandemic fallout. Traditional dating was disrupted by COVID, which had knock-on effects of fewer Americans getting engaged in the following years. For Signet, this was disastrous, as their main product, bridal jewelry, wasn't selling with fewer people falling in love. Signet's sales and earnings plummeted, with the stock falling nearly 40% from 2024 to mid-2025.

However, that seemed to turn around since mid-2025, as the stock has climbed with the number of engagements making a gradual recovery. But more importantly, the underlying business has made considerable developments. With a new CEO and the adoption of lab-grown diamonds, I believe the market is not pricing in the full upside of underlying business strength, with the stock trading at 11x forward earnings. For those reasons, I believe Signet is a Buy.

Background

Signet operates nearly 2,600 stores worldwide under a few brands, most notably Kay Jewelers, Zales, Jared, and a few others. Like I mentioned, Signet's main product is bridal jewelry, which accounts for nearly 50% of company revenue. Signet also holds a 30% share of the overall U.S. bridal jewelry market, worth nearly $10 billion. So when engagement numbers dwindled, Signet was hurt disproportionately more than its competitors.

By fiscal Q4 2024 (ended February 2024), the company had seen same-store sales decline 9.6%, while total revenue dropped 6.3% to $2.5 billion. Then CEO Virginia Drosos announced that engagements had indeed reached a trough, but the company expected a gradual three-year recovery.

To put its performance against competitors in context, FY 2025 sales fell 6.5% for Signet. Pandora (PNDRY), which has minimal bridal exposure, grew revenue 13%, while LVMH's watch and jewelry division fell only 2%. Mastercard data also showed that the jewelry industry grew 4% during the 2024 holiday period, whereas Signet's comps were down 2% during this time.

When Signet missed 2024 holiday expectations, the stock seemed to be plummeting. But the structural business changes were already underway with the then newly appointed CEO, J.K. Symancyk, who had three decades of experience across the retail industry. And so, the company slowly started to embark on its turnaround.

Strategy

Symancyk's strategy, called "Grow Brand Love," is focused on three areas. First, shifting the company from a banner to a brand mindset. Symancyk said in an interview that customer awareness is already high for brands such as Kay and Zales, but there's little overlap between the customer bases. Positioning brands (Kay: milestone and romantic, Zales: Style and trend, Jared: inspired luxury, etc.) would sharpen the distinction between the smaller brands under Signet that don't have as much customer awareness.

Secondly, Signet is expanding beyond its core bridal offerings into adjacent categories such as fashion and everyday jewelry, where it only holds a low single-digit market share currently. This is where lab-grown diamonds (LGDs) become super important. LGDs currently account for 15% of Signet's fashion sales (2x the rate YoY), while making up 40% of bridal band sales. LGDs are attractive due to their higher gross margins of 60-65% compared to natural diamonds (40-45%). Although the dollar value per LGD unit is lower, the margin expansion goes hand-in-hand with Signet's larger variety of offerings at accessible prices.

Lastly, within the next three years, Signet aims to optimize its real estate by transitioning 10% of its mall locations to off-mall and e-commerce channels. Given that the average mall lease term is over two years, management is provided with plenty of flexibility to execute this shift without being locked into underperforming locations for an extended time. Through Q3 fiscal 2026, management was able to cut 35 stores, continuing to decrease fixed costs.

Recent Performance

Under the new strategy, Signet has now posted three consecutive quarters of positive same-store sales growth.

Period (Fiscal 2026) Same-Store Sales Growth

Q1

 +2.5%
Q2 +2%
Q3 +3%

Brand-specific seems to be gaining traction too, with core brands (Kay, Zales, and Jared) outperforming, combining for a 5% and 6% comps increase in Q2 and Q3, respectively.

From an expectations standpoint, Q3 was pretty exceptional. Revenue came in at $1.39 billion (vs. $1.36 billion expectations), and adjusted EPS also was strong at $0.63 (vs. $0.16 expectations). But notably, gross margin grew 130 bps (merchandise margins improved 30 bps) to 37.3%. In a troubling environment where gold costs surged 30% YoY and there was an increase in India import tariffs from 10% to 50% on roughly half of merchandised purchases, Signet was able to stick to its pricing position and benefit from its strategy implementations.

Catalysts

Besides engagement recovery, I think the key to underpriced catalysts lies in the following.

Lab-Grown Diamonds

Some view LGDs as a headwind due to lower average selling prices. Roughly two years ago, the narrative around LGDs was that they were cutting into natural diamond sales and hurting overall revenue. That narrative has seemed to change. As I mentioned before, LGD penetration doubled YoY to 15% for Signet. Management has made investments into LGD products below $1,000. Recently, the street updated price targets to reflect this, but I believe further development of LGD products will continue to drive margin expansion and an increase in EPS, with upside potential not fully captured as of yet.

Valentine's Day Tailwinds

The NRF projected Valentine's Day spending of $29.1 billion in 2026, up 6% from 2025's $27.5 billion. By dollars spent, Jewelry was the top category for the 10th year in a row, with consumers expected to spend $7 billion, up from $6.5 billion in 2025. Due to its outstanding market share, Signet is positioned to capture an outsized portion of this spending.

Buybacks

In Q1 fiscal 2026 alone, Signet repurchased roughly 2.1 million shares ($117 million) and has $600 million in buyback authorization remaining. With preferred shares fully redeemed, capital seems to be returning to shareholders through buybacks and a growing dividend ($1.28 yearly, ~1.3% yield) amid a rebounding engagement environment.

Valuation

At the current price of shares, I see the multiple trading at its prior Q4 levels at 7.1x, a bit above street expectations of 6.8x, considering four quarters of positive comp growth, expanding margins, and strong cash flow generation since its 2024 trough. As for the price decline in the past week, I believe this is the market overweighting cautionary guidance issued last quarter ahead of next week's earnings report. Unless I see an extremely weak earnings report, which I suppose is unlikely, the current price looks like a strategic entry point as it trades below current street expectations.

Risks

Consumer Weakness

Given the discretionary nature of jewelry, if the macro environment deteriorates or consumer confidence slows down, same-store sales growth would suffer.

Tariffs and Gold

Gold costs were up 30% YoY as of Q2, and the Russia-related tariff penalty on Indian imports raised the rate from 10% to 50%, as mentioned before. Although management has done well so far, if these pressures intensify, margin expansion could stall.

Conclusion

With earnings only a few days away, a strong quarter would increase guidance and push the stock up. Given that I find Signet benefiting from structural improvements, notably LGDs and brand strategy, I would like to get in while the engagement recovery factor is still at play.
